CanonicalTitle: AgentOps in the Real World: Running Multi-Agent Systems You Can Actually Trust
AbstractShort: What it takes to run a multi-agent system in production: observability, idempotency, role-scoped auth, and the failure modes nobody warns you about.
AbstractLong: Most agent demos stop where production begins. This talk picks up where the demo ends. We walk through a real multi-agent system — discovery, verification, scoring, and tracker maintenance — running on Microsoft Agent Framework and Azure AI Foundry, and look at the operational backbone: OpenTelemetry traces, App Insights dashboards, managed-identity auth, idempotent writes, and the half-dozen failure modes (clock skew, partial batch writes, model drift, retry storms) that turn a clever demo into a 2 AM page. You leave with a checklist you can hand to the team on Monday.

CanonicalTitle: Hybrid Agents: Where Copilot Studio Stops and Pro-Code Starts
AbstractShort: Low-code Copilot Studio agents and pro-code Microsoft Agent Framework agents are not rivals — they are layers. Here is where each one earns its keep.
AbstractLong: Every enterprise AI team eventually hits the same fork: keep going with Copilot Studio, or escalate to pro-code on Microsoft Agent Framework? The honest answer is both, in the right places. This session draws the line. We look at three real workloads — a tier-1 helpdesk router, a CFP discovery pipeline, and a contract redlining flow — and walk through which platform fits each layer, how data flows across the seam, and what governance looks like when low-code and pro-code agents share the same Azure AI Foundry model fabric.

CanonicalTitle: Microsoft 365 Copilot: From Request to Retirement
AbstractShort: A governance lifecycle for Microsoft 365 Copilot agents — from intake request through retirement, with the controls auditors actually ask for.
AbstractLong: Microsoft 365 Copilot agents are easy to spin up and hard to govern. This talk lays out a full lifecycle: how an intake request becomes a vetted agent, how access scoping and DLP labels stay coherent in production, how usage telemetry feeds a quarterly review, and how an agent is retired without leaving orphaned data behind. You leave with a one-page lifecycle diagram, a sample intake form, and the Purview and Entra controls that anchor each phase.

CanonicalTitle: Azure AI Foundry for Enterprise: Practical Patterns Beyond the Playground
AbstractShort: What changes when Azure AI Foundry leaves the playground and enters production: networking, identity, observability, cost, and the patterns that hold up under audit.
AbstractLong: Most Foundry demos run in an open subscription with public endpoints and a personal API key. Enterprise reality has private endpoints, conditional access, customer-managed keys, and a finance team that wants to know where every token went. This talk maps the gap. We work through five patterns that move a Foundry workload from playground to production: private networking, managed-identity auth, OTel-based cost attribution, prompt-and-response auditing, and a deployment topology that survives a region failover.
